name: Galician

ancestors.notes: Galaico-Portuguese: the common ancestor. Galician diverged from Portuguese in the 14th c. after the Portuguese court moved to Lisbon and Galicia came under increasing Castilian political control.
Gallaecian Celtic substrate, strong in northwest Iberia. Possible influences on prosody and nasal vowel development.

Asturleonese/Leonese adstrate: after political separation from Portugal, Galicia remained within the Crown of Castile/León. Castilian/Leonese influence intensified throughout the late medieval and early modern period, contributing to: betacism (v→b), z=/z/ retention, ⟨ll⟩ and ⟨ñ⟩ orthographic conventions (Castilian over Portuguese lh/nh), and loss of some Galaico-Portuguese features.
Suebi superstrate: inherited through Galaico-Portuguese, centered in Gallaecia.
Andalusi Arabic: limited contact in the north, some lexical items through Castilian intermediary.
Occitan: troubadour influence on medieval Galician-Portuguese lyric poetry.
Lusitanian substrate: pre-Celtic IE of western Iberia, inherited through Galaico-Portuguese.

notes: Standard Galician (galego) per the Real Academia Galega (RAG) *Normas ortográficas e morfolóxicas do idioma galego* (2003, revised 2012). Spoken by ~2.4 million people in the Autonomous Community of Galicia (Spain) and diaspora communities. KEY FEATURES vs. Portuguese: (1) DISTINCIÓN (interdental /θ/): standard Galician HAS the phoneme /θ/ — ⟨c⟩ before e/i and ⟨z⟩ = /θ/ (as in Castilian; Portuguese has no interdental, using /s/ vs /z/). SESEO — the merger of /θ/ into a lamino-alveolar [s] (⟨c/z⟩ = /s/) — is a WESTERN/coastal DIALECTAL feature, NOT part of the RAG standard; see gl-x-occidental. Regueira (1996: 119) describes standard Galician with /θ/ and explicitly notes it does not show the dialectal seseo or gheada. (2) APICO-ALVEOLAR /s/ [s̺] like Castilian/Asturian (not Portuguese predorsal [s̻]). (3) BETACISM: ⟨b⟩ = ⟨v⟩ = /b/ (unlike Portuguese which preserves /v/ ≠ /b/). (4) ⟨ll⟩ = /ʎ/, ⟨ñ⟩ = /ɲ/ (Castilian orthographic conventions, not Portuguese ⟨lh⟩/⟨nh⟩). (5) ⟨ch⟩ = /tʃ/ (standard pronunciation; urban registers increasingly /ʃ/ under Portuguese/Spanish influence). (6) ⟨x⟩ = /ʃ/ — covers many words where Castilian uses ⟨j⟩/⟨ge,gi⟩ (e.g. 'xeneral' [ʃeneˈɾal] vs. Spanish 'general'). (7) NO VOWEL REDUCTION: unstressed /a/ stays [a] (not /ɐ/ as in Portuguese). (8) 7 oral vowel qualities in tonic position: /a, e, ɛ, i, o, ɔ, u/. (9) NASAL VOWELS word-finally (irmán [ɐ̃], corazón [õ]). (10) Preserves diphthongs: ou /ow/ ('cousa'), ei /ej/ ('leite'). KEY FEATURES vs. Asturian: standard Galician (like standard Asturian) has interdental /θ/; it lacks Leonese diphthongization of Latin Ĕ/Ŏ in the systematic way Asturleonese shows, and descends from Galaico-Portuguese not Asturleonese. Sources: Freixeiro Mato (2006) *Gramática da Lingua Galega*, Regueira (1996), Ferreiro (1997).

sources.author: Carballo Calero, R.
sources.title: Gramática elemental del gallego común (7th ed.)

sources.author: Regueira, X.L.
sources.title: Galician (Illustrations of the IPA)
sources.publisher: Journal of the International Phonetic Association 26(2)
sources.notes: Standard Galician illustration. p.119: consonant inventory includes interdental /θ/; states standard Galician does not show the western-dialectal seseo (/θ/,/ʃ/ merged to [s]) or gheada (/ɡ/→[h~x~χ]). Confirms distinción is the standard, seseo is dialectal.

sources.author: Gamallo, P., Regueira, X.L. & others
sources.title: Cotovia TTS System (GTM, Universidade de Vigo)
sources.notes: Stress rules derived from the Cotovia front-end acentuar_prosodicamente() implementation (sil_acen.cpp).

stress.notes: Galician accentuation as implemented in the Cotovia front-end (GTM, Universidade de Vigo): written accent wins; words ending in a vowel, -n or -s are paroxytone (casa, cantan, casas); words ending in any other consonant (r l z x d) are oxytone (falar, azul, rapaz). CLITICS: cliticless_words lists the Galician palabras atonas (Freixeiro Mato 1998, Gramatica da lingua galega, prosodia; RAG/ILG Normas; Regueira 1996) — the articles o/a/os/as, indefinite un/unha(+plurals), the preposition+article contractions ao/do/no/co (and variants), the atonic prepositions de/en/con/por/sen/a/para, the conjunctions e/ou/nin/que/se/mais, and the weak pronouns me/te/se/lle/lles/nos/vos/o/a/os/as/che and combined forms. The polo/pola contractions are TONIC and are deliberately excluded (they bear stress: pola [ˈpɔla]).

orthography_standard.name: Normas ortográficas e morfolóxicas do idioma galego
orthography_standard.authority: Real Academia Galega / Instituto da Lingua Galega
orthography_standard.notes: 23rd edition (revised). Defines the standard spelling; seseo and gheada are dialectal, not normative.

allophone_rules.notes: Galician tonic ⟨o⟩ is open-mid [ɔ] in these lexemes, unmarked in RAG spelling (⟨ó⟩ is not written on them): corre [ˈkɔre], the tonic contraction pola [ˈpɔla] (Regueira 1996; Freixeiro Mato 1998; the gl-015 arbitration). Pins ONLY the stressed vowel; 'pola' is tonic (declared outside cliticless_words) so it also keeps its stress mark.
